response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a retired man who's always been skeptical about new things, I've seen my fair share of misinformation throughout my life. Social media is no different from what happened back in the day when people used newspapers or word-of-mouth for news. But times have changed dramatically with this digital age we're living in now. It seems like every week there's another story circulating online that could be true, half-true, or completely false - all mixed together without any clear way to tell them apart.

I remember when I was growing up, my father didn't even finish high school but he always managed to get the facts straight from reading his newspaper daily. Now, looking at social media, it feels like everyone has an opinion and a platform to share it regardless of accuracy. That's where being open-minded comes into play - because I'm moderately open (with that 46 openness score), I try not to dismiss everything outright but neither do I believe every wild claim floating around.

Perhaps what helped me most is simply taking things with a grain of salt, just like my mother taught me about baking measurements. You don't take the ingredients at face value without measuring properly. Similarly, when something pops up on social media, you should fact-check it using reliable sources. My wife tells me I'm sometimes too cautious - and maybe she's right given my low neuroticism score of just 3! But honestly? Better to be safe than sorry.

And let's not forget about education: while mine is a bachelor's degree (nothing fancy like the PhD my son has), knowing how to analyze information properly helps immensely. I still enjoy running and photography too, but those hobbies don't involve dealing with fake content directly unless you're framing it that way!
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I approach this topic as an older individual accustomed to traditional media where fact-checking was part of the process. With my high extraversion (100 score) but moderate agreeableness (38), I believe in engaging with others while maintaining critical judgment. Since social media is different from print newspapers or even television, it requires developing new habits like cross-referencing information and not trusting everything at first glance.

My low neuroticism helps me stay calm when encountering dubious content, avoiding panic that would lead to spreading misinformation further. As a retired person spending only occasional time on social media (a few times weekly), I don't feel the pressure to be constantly updated, which frees up mental space for careful evaluation.
